invariant_definition: Part, portion, or share. In Vedānta the term becomes a controlled way of saying that a dependent reality belongs to *brahman* or Bhagavān without immediately collapsing into strict identity. It therefore sits beside *prakāra*, *śakti*, *apṛthak-siddhi*, and the many forms of *bhedābheda*. A major scriptural driver is *Bhagavad-Gītā* 15.7, *mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke jīvabhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ*: the eternal *jīva* is 'my very own *aṃśa*.' Schools then ask what sort of partness this can be. Is the self a literal part of the Lord, a mode inseparable from Him, a dependent energy, or only figuratively called a part because of similarity or subordination? Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavas distinguish *svāṃśa* manifestations of Bhagavān from *vibhinnāṃśa* selves; Bhāskara reads partness through real transformation; Advaita refuses literal partness because *brahman* is partless; Tattva-vāda (the school often labeled Dvaita) rejects it because the *jīva* is eternally distinct. 'Part' is thus never enough by itself; one must specify the metaphysics of the whole.

school_framing.shared_core: *Aṃśa* names **a part: a constituent that stands in a part-whole relation to its whole**. The structural role is shared. Vedāntic schools dispute the *jīva*'s status as *aṃśa* of Brahman: Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda treat the *jīva* as real *aṃśa*; Nimbārka and Vallabha treat the *jīva* as *svābhāvika* *aṃśa* / *aṃśa-śakti*; Advaita treats the *jīva* as *aṃśa* of Brahman only *aupacārikally*, since Brahman is *niravayava*.
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** (AF1) on whether Brahman can have *aṃśas* at all. Same M-register, same scope-inside. Advaita reads BS 2.3.43 (*aṃśo nānā-vyapadeśāt*) as a *pedagogical* surface; theistic Vedāntas read the same sūtra as a constructive M-thesis. Methodology Cases 3–5.

per_school.definition: Since duality is only *māyāmātra* and *advaita* alone is *paramārtha*, scriptural part-language cannot mark a real partition within brahman [[MK 1.17](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.17)]. When the unborn reality is known, *dvaita* is no longer found, so any *aṃśa* discourse must belong to the pedagogical and empirical register opened by beginningless *māyā*, not to brahman's own nature [[MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)].

Rāmānuja's proem already places *aṃśa* within the *antaryāmin* grammar: the śrutis teach cit and acit as the Lord's *śakti*, *aṃśa*, *vibhūti*, *rūpa*, and *śarīra* under a single *sāmānādhikaraṇya* [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. In this usage, *aṃśa* denotes a real but inseparable mode of Bhagavān Nārāyaṇa, governed by *śarīra-śarīrin-bhāva* rather than by any detachable mereology [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; prapanna [REAL-DISAGREEMENT: jīva as real aṃśa]

per_school.definition: For Madhva, the *sākṣin* that apprehends difference is itself a foundational *pramāṇa*, so the distinction between *jīva* and Īśvara is *śrutisāmarthya-susthira* and not available for collapse into identity [[Anuvy. 2.3.66](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66)]. The abheda-texts are retained only on the basis of *sādṛśya*: consciousness and bliss similar to the Lord remain in the liberated *jīva*, and scriptural non-difference-language therefore works by likeness and dependence, never by *svarūpābhidā* [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. In the *aṃśa-adhikaraṇa*, then, *aṃśa* is read through asymmetric dependence rather than through any essential sharing of the Lord's being [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)].

Jīva Gosvāmī coordinates the *abheda-śāstras* by holding that the aggregate of *jīvas* stands to Bhagavān, through his *svābhāvika acintya-śakti*, in the position of atomic rays to the sun [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. On that basis, *vyatireka* and *avyatireka* are jointly maintained, so *aṃśa* is to be understood through dependent radiance and *śakti*, not through crude severable parthood [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Bhagavān is consequently the *parama-svarūpa* of all, as the sun is of its rays, which fixes the devotional completion of this ontology [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

Śrīnivāsa states the school position with full symmetry of denial: the *jīva* is neither *atyantabhinna* nor *atyantābhinna* with respect to Śrī Puruṣottama, but *paramātmano 'ṃśaḥ* [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. That *aṃśa* is explicitly glossed as *śaktirūpa*, and the *jīva* is accordingly described as *alpaśakti* and *asvatantra* [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. The term therefore secures real difference and non-difference within an asymmetrical dependence-structure, rather than licensing mutual convertibility of *jīva* and Īśvara [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)].

Vallabha states without attenuation that the *jīva* is *brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ*, grounding the claim in *nānāvyapadeśa* and confirming it from Bhagavad-Gītā 15.7 [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. At the same time, whether Brahman is to be called *niraṃśa* or *sāṃśa* is not to be settled from ordinary experience at all, since Brahman is *vedaika-samadhigamya* [[BSB 2.3.43-45c](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45c)]. *Aṃśa* here names a real manifestation of Brahman disclosed by śruti and smṛti, not a merely figurative or subtractive partition [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)][[BSB 2.3.43-45c](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45c)].

In Sāṃkhya the manifest (*vyakta*) is expressly *sāvayava*, while the unmanifest source is its contrary; part-structure therefore belongs to produced multiplicity rather than to the causal equilibrium of *avyakta* [[SK 10](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/10)]. The school's use of part-language is thus ontological rather than merely lexical: what is *hetumat*, *anekam*, *āśritam*, and *paratantra* is also what is composite, whereas the causal source is *niravayava* [[SK 10](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/10)].

In Vyāsa's analysis of *bhūtajaya*, *dravya* is a *samūha* carried by differentiated *avayava*s and *bhāga*s, and the yogin's *saṃyama* proceeds by penetrating this layered constitution of the elements [[YBh 3.44](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.44)]. Part-language here is technical: both *yutasiddha* and *ayutasiddha* composites are distinguished, and even the atomic-tanmātric level is described through avayava-structure within the analysis of the bhūtas [[YBh 3.44](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.44)].

Nyāya refuses the reduction of perception to bare part-apprehension: although an opponent appeals to *ekadeśagrahaṇa*, the sūtras reply that the whole (*avayavin*) is real and is established precisely through such perception, as shown by support and traction [[NS 2.1.31-37](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.31-37)]. Later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika states the relation through *samavāya*: part and whole are *ayutasiddha*, since one member cannot remain as what it is apart from its inseparable dependence on the other [[Tarka-Saṅgraha 79](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/79)].

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, part-language functions hermeneutically within the preservation of *vākyaikya*: where multiple guṇas would otherwise fracture a unitary injunction, praise may proceed 'through a part to the whole' (*aṃśadvārā aṃśisaṃstutiḥ*) [[JNM 1.4.35](cite://madhava/jaimini-nyaya-mala/1.4.35)]. The point is not a free-standing mereology, but the control of *vākyabheda*: a subordinate feature can commend the principal rite without generating additional, independent enjoinments [[JNM 1.4.35](cite://madhava/jaimini-nyaya-mala/1.4.35)].

In Nāgārjuna, *ekadeśa*-reasoning serves as a *prasaṅga* against mixed ontological predication: if something were divine in one part and human in another, or finite in one part and infinite in another, incompatible determinations would attach to a single supposed entity [[MMK 27.17-26](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/27.17-26)]. The school's engagement with part-language is therefore aporetic: partition into portions cannot rescue *svabhāva* by distributing contrary states across the same thing [[MMK 27.17-26](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/27.17-26)].

Vasubandhu turns *aṃśa-bhāga* reasoning against atomism: if a paramāṇu can stand in simultaneous relation to six directions, it must have six parts, but once directional differentiation is admitted, strict atomic unity collapses [[Viṃśikā 12-14](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/12-14)]. The point is epistemological and ontological at once: divisibility shows that the imagined external atom cannot serve as the ultimately real basis of perceptual extension [[Viṃśikā 12-14](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/12-14)].

Kṣemarāja explains the contracted state of consciousness by saying that, even when veiled in māyā, *citi* still scorches and appropriates its object only 'by a part' (*aṃśena*), not yet through total reabsorption [[PH 14](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/14)]. Here *aṃśa* marks a limited manifestation of the one *cit-śakti*: contraction is partial self-assimilation within consciousness itself, not the severance of a detachable piece from an external whole [[PH 14](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/14)].

Jain ontology articulates partness through *pradeśa* and *bhāga*: dharma, adharma, and each individual jīva possess innumerable spatial units, while pudgala is divisible into one, numerable, and innumerable regions [[TS, pradeśa section](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pradesa-bhajya-pradipavat)]. The jīva's occupation of extension is then described by contraction and expansion of those pradeśas 'like a lamp', so part-structure is intrinsic to embodied substances without cancelling their persistence as dravyas [[TS, pradeśa section](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pradesa-bhajya-pradipavat)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: The Pāṇinian-Vaiyākaraṇa tradition certainly uses technical notions such as *ekadeśa* in grammar, but *aṃśa* is not, in the present doctrinal sense, a school-defining metaphysical topic; and the requisite primary grammatical corpus is not on disk.

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'part.' In English that usually suggests a detachable piece cut from a larger whole. Most Sanskrit debates are precisely about refusing that picture while still preserving dependence, derivation, or manifestation. Translating *aṃśa* without noting whether the school means mode, power, transformation, or only figurative belonging imports a crude mereology the texts are arguing over.
